coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional visible light photocatalytic carbon dots synergize with reactive oxygen species for anti-quorum sensing and anti-bacteria for salmon preservation

coredata.dc:description: Quorum sensing (QS)-induced spoilage causes approximately 15–20% of annual food wastage. However, traditional natural QS inhibitors exhibit poor water solubility and no catalytic activity. Therefore, this study aimed to synthesize novel anti-QS carbon dots (RR-CDs) with good water solubility, visible light photocatalytic activity, and high biocompatibility. The RR-CDs (<200 μg mL−1) exhibited considerable broad-spectrum antibacterial activity by generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) to disrupt the cell membrane wall structure. Owing to the appropriate bandgap of RR-CDs, a high ROS content, including · O2 −, 1O2, and · OH, was generated via energy transfer under visible light irradiation. Furthermore, the combined effect of RR-CDs and ROS reduced the biofilm formation, swarming, swimming, and extracellular protease production of Pseudomonas fluorescens by 48.45%, 25.51%, 52.79%, and 38.67%, respectively, by inhibiting LuxI/R expression, which further decreased pgaC, tyrB, motB, and aprE expression by >50 %. Notably, RR-CDs exhibited no obvious cytotoxicity; furthermore, they could extend the shelf life of salmon by 3 d. Altogether, these results present and validate the strong ability of photocatalytic RR-CDs for QS inhibition and show their potential in aquatic product preservation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis, anti-QS mechanism, antibacterial mechanism, and application of RR-CDs.

Synthesis and Description of RR-CDs. a) FTIR spectra of RR-CDs. b) TEM and HRTEM images of RR-CDs. c) XRD images of RR-CDs. d) XPS full images of RR-CDs. e) UV–Vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence spectra, and images of RR-CDs solution.

Anti-bacteria activity of RR-CDs. a) Plate results of bacteriostatic tests. b) Plot of CLSM results of bacterial inhibition test. Scale bar: 10 µm. c) Morphological observation of P. fluorescens after visible light, RR-CDs, and different concentrations of RR-CDs (visible light) treatments. Scale bar: 1 µm. Effects of visible light, RR-CDs, and different concentrations of RR-CDs (visible light) on the growth curves (d) and cell membrane permeability (e) of P. fluorescens. f) Antibacterial mechanism of RR-CDs in visible light.

Photocatalytic mechanism of RR-CDs. a) UV–vis DRS and band-gap energies of RR-CDs. PL spectroscopy (b), transient photocurrent densities (c), EIS image (d), and CV curves (e) of RR-CDs, Rose Bengal, and riboflavin. f) Mott-Schottky curves of RR-CDs. The ·O2 – (g), 1O2 (h), and ·OH (i) were determined by EPR. j) Measurement of RR-CDs vacancies. k) Measurement of RR-CDs hole-electron. l) Schematic diagram for the possible ROS production for RR-CDs under visible light irradiation.

Electron transfer pathways in RR-CDs. The free energy diagrams of (a) 1O2, and ·O2 −, and (b) ·OH generation on RR-CDs. The initial state is set to be 0 eV. b) Proposed catalytic mechanism of (c) 1O2, and (d) ·OH conversion on RR-CDs. The brown, silver, red, violet, green, and white balls represent C, N, O, I, Cl, and H atoms, respectively. The charge density differences of the (e) adsorption of O2 on RR-CDs, and (f) adsorption of H2O on RR-CDs. The cyan area represents electron depletion, and the yellow area indicates electron accumulation.

Anti-QS properties of RR-CDs. a) Suppression of QS by RR-CDs. Effects of RR-CDs on the P. fluorescens (b) and CV026 (c). Effects of C6-HSL, visible light, RR-CDs, and RR-CDs (visible light) on the swarming (d), swimming (e), and ECP (f) of P. fluorescens.

Anti-QS properties of RR-CDs. a) Biofilm formation status after dealing with different dealing groups. Scale bar: 2 μm. b) CLSM images of biofilm formation. Scale bar: 50 μm. Crystal violet staining images (c) and quantitative analysis (d) of biofilm formation. e) Mechanism diagram of QS inhibition by RR-CDs.

The effect of RR-CDs on QS at the molecular level. a) Demonstration of docking of small molecules to LuxI/LuxR proteins molecular docking modeling. b) qPCR analysis of RR-CDs against genes of P. fluorescens. c) Mechanism of QS inhibition by RR-CDs.

Application of the RR-CDs (visible light) in inhibiting salmon fillets spoilage. a) Morphological changes of salmon fillets treatment by Light, RR-CDs, RosA, and RR-CDs (visible light). Scale bar: 2 cm. b) Tissue fiber changes of salmon fillets treatment by Light, RR-CDs, RosA, and RR-CDs (visible light). Scale bar: 50 μm. TVC (c), pH (d), TVB-N (e), and TBA (f) of salmon fillets treatment by Light, RR-CDs, RosA, and RR-CDs (visible light). g) The preservation mechanism of RR-CDs on salmon fillets under visible light.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quorum sensing (QS)-induced spoilage causes approximately 15–20% of annual food wastage. However, traditional natural QS inhibitors exhibit poor water solubility and no catalytic activity. Therefore, this study aimed to synthesize novel anti-QS carbon dots (RR-CDs) with good water solubility, visible light photocatalytic activity, and high biocompatibility. The RR-CDs (<200 μg mL−1) exhibited considerable broad-spectrum antibacterial activity by generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) to disrupt the cell membrane wall structure. Owing to the appropriate bandgap of RR-CDs, a high ROS content, including · O2 −, 1O2, and · OH, was generated via energy transfer under visible light irradiation. Furthermore, the combined effect of RR-CDs and ROS reduced the biofilm formation, swarming, swimming, and extracellular protease production of Pseudomonas fluorescens by 48.45%, 25.51%, 52.79%, and 38.67%, respectively, by inhibiting LuxI/R expression, which further decreased pgaC, tyrB, motB, and aprE expression by >50 %. Notably, RR-CDs exhibited no obvious cytotoxicity; furthermore, they could extend the shelf life of salmon by 3 d. Altogether, these results present and validate the strong ability of photocatalytic RR-CDs for QS inhibition and show their potential in aquatic product preservation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food spoilage results in approximately 15–20% of annual food wastage, with microbial-mediated quorum sensing (QS) being the main cause [1] . Pseudomonas fluorescens (P. fluorescens) is known to spoil various refrigerated aquatic products by secreting extracellular proteases (ECPs), which cause protein degradation in aquatic products and dense biofilm formation [2,3]. These factors exacerbate the spoilage of aquatic products, leading to considerable waste. Furthermore, the biofilm formation increases the infectivity and antibiotic resistance of the bacterium [4]. Therefore, selecting suitable bacteriostatic and QS-inhibiting materials is essential to minimize bacterial biofilm-mediated infections and prolong the preservation period of aquatic products.

QS serves as an interbacterial communication mechanism and is instrumental in eliciting various biological functions [5]. Extracellular signal molecules, namely autoinducers, are the prime modulators of QS. The autoinducers accumulate with increasing bacterial density, and after reaching the threshold, they are detected by receptors and subsequently affect gene expression, thereby inducing protease secretion and biofilm formation [6]. P. fluorescens also utilizes QS via its OS system, which allows it to grow even under unfavorable conditions, such as low temperatures. Targeting QS represents an effective strategy for mitigating bacterial resistance over antibacterial approaches. A viable approach in this strategy is the utilization of QS inhibitors (QSIs), which can disrupt QS-regulated bacterial activities and minimize drug resistance.

Carbon dots (CDs), a novel kind of water-soluble and biocompatible carbon-based nanomaterials, present a broad antibacterial application prospect. Owing to their unique photosensitivity and low cytotoxicity, CDs have garnered considerable attention in studies related to photodynamic therapy (PDT) [7,8]. Photosensitizers eliminate bacteria by generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) through different pathways following exposure to light [9]. For example, under near-infrared irradiation, the AI-MPDA nanoplatform proposed by Yuan produced ROS and triggered the release of NO to eliminate biofilms [10]. However, the mechanisms underlying CD-mediated QS inhibition have not been systematically studied.

Therefore, QS-inhibiting photocatalytic RR-CDs with bacteriostatic activity were synthesized using Rose Bengal and riboflavin. Synthesized RR-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD). Furthermore, the photocatalytic activity and ROS generation were characterized by examining the photoelectric properties. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) were performed to evaluate the antibacterial activity of RR-CDs. Furthermore, molecular docking was performed to investigate the interaction between RR-CDs and LuxI/R. The synergistic effects of RR-CDs and light on the motility, extracellular protease, and biofilm inhibition of P. fluorescens were assessed through quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qPCR) (Fig. 1 ). Additionally, the corruption-inhibition ability of RR-CDs on salmon fillets was evaluated via the freshness test of salmon fillets. The cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) experiment was performed to determine the toxicity of RR-CDs. The findings of this study may provide the potential of the novel RR-CDs developed in this study as a QSI for preserving aquatic products.

Rose Bengal, riboflavin, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide, 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-1-piperidine, ethanol, glutaraldehyde, fructose, NaCl, thiobarbituric acid, and an anti-fluorescence quenching sealing agent were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Corp (Shanghai, China). The Luria–Bertani (LB) medium, fluorescein diacetate-acetone, N-hexanoyl-L-Homoserine lactone (C6-HSL), agar, kanamycin monosulfate, crystal violet, and glacial acetic acid were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (Shanghai, China) Trading Co., Ltd. EZ-10 Total RNA Mini-PrECP Kit and primers were purchased from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China) Co., Ltd. BeyoFast™ SYBR Green One-Step qRT-PCR Kit was purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology (China). Live/Dead BacLight Bacterial Viability Kit (L7012), 3–5,2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein-diacetate (DCFH-DA), peptone, and tryptone were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (China) Co., Ltd. The AO/PI double staining kit was purchased from Bjbalb (Beijing, China) Technology Co., Ltd. The development and maintenance of bacterial culture are described in the Supplementary Material.

RR-CDs were prepared by a simple one-step method. Following their dissolution in 10 mL of water, Rose Bengal (0.25 g) and riboflavin (0.25 g) were added to a reaction kettle for a 3-h reaction at 180°C. Reacted liquid was then filtered to remove impurities and further purified by dialysis in a 1500-Da dialysis bag for about 24 h. Upon completion, the solution was lyophilized by freeze-drying, and the resulting powder was the desired CD.

The RR-CDs solution was dropped onto the copper mesh and tested by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). The D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Brook, Germany) was used to analyze the X-ray diffraction (XRD) of RR-CDs. The functional group of the RR-CDs was determined by FTIR (Nicolet 6700, USA). The RR-CDs were mixed with potassium bromide at a ratio of 1:100, ground, pressed, and placed in the instrument for scanning. XPS with thermo scalable 250 XI (USA) was used to record the observations. The samples were subjected to UV–Vis (range: 200–700 nm) using the UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Japan). The fluorescence emission spectra of the samples were obtained in the range of 300–600 nm by F7000 c (Japan). The experimental details are described in the Supporting Information.

Referring to Wang’s method and modified it slightly [11]. A solution of RR-CDs was placed under continuous irradiation in visible light (220 V, 4 W light strips) and the fluorescence spectra of RR-CDs were recorded at various times. The RR-CDs solution was mixed with multiple ions and organics (glucose, leucine, histidine, Mn2+, Ca2+, Na+, K+, NH4 +, Cl-), the mixture was stirred for 5 min and the fluorescence spectra of the mixture were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of bacterial growth on plates
ce:section.ce:para: We referred to Cui’s method and modified it slightly [12]. To assess the antibacterial activity, different bacteria were used as model microorganisms, which were cultured overnight and diluted to a specific concentration in the logarithmic phase (OD600 = 0.1) before determining the antibacterial activity of the samples. Diluted bacterial suspensions were treated differently. Subsequently, 100 μL of each sample was added to LB plates using the plate method and fostered at 28°C for 12 h. Different treatment groups were as follows: Different bacteria without RR-CDs (control), bacteria with RR-CDs (200 μg mL−1), bacteria with visible light irradiation, and RR-CDs (25, 50, 100, and 200 μg mL−1).

We referred to Sun’s method and modified it slightly [13]. After cultivating for the entire night, 1 mL of P. fluorescens was taken and centrifuged for 5 min at 8554 g in a centrifuge tube. After removing the supernatant, the precipitate of P. fluorescens was again suspended in a 0.9% NaCl solution. The experiment was repeated thrice to collect P. fluorescens suspension. A total of 200 μL of the differently treated bacterial solutions were taken in a 96-well plate, and the growth of the bacteria was recorded using a growth curve meter. The data were recorded every 2 h, measurements were taken for 48 h, and the experiment was repeated thrice for each sample. Different treatment groups were as follows: P. fluorescens without visible light (220 V, 4 W light strips) irradiation and RR-CDs (control), P. fluorescens with visible light, P. fluorescens with RR-CDs (100 μg mL−1), and P. fluorescens with visible light irradiation and RR-CDs (25, 50, and 100 μg mL−1).

We referred to Xu’s method and modified it slightly [14]. The intracellular levels of ROS were quantified using DCFH-DA. In total, 1 mL of P. fluorescens cultured overnight was treated differently (The treatment method is the same as that used in the growth curve of P. fluorescens experiment). Following 3 h of culturing the P. fluorescens suspension treated differently in a shaker at 28°C and 160 rpm, DCFH-DA (10 mmol/L) was added to each treatment group at a dilution ratio of 1:1000, and further incubated in the shaker half an hour. Subsequently, the bacterial suspension was centrifuged at 10,000 g for 5 min, followed by resuspension. The fluorescence intensity of the bacterial suspension was measured at excitation and emission wavelengths of 488 nm and 525 nm, respectively.

We referred to Lan’s method and modified it slightly [15]. In total, 1 mL of P. fluorescens cultured overnight was treated differently (The treatment method is the same as that used in the growth curve of P. fluorescens experiment). The P. fluorescens suspension treated differently was cultured overnight and centrifuged, and the bacterial precipitate was collected. Later, 250 μL of the fluorescein diacetate-acetone solution was added to the precipitate and maintained at 25°C. The mixture was centrifuged at the end of the resting period, followed by resuspension in a phosphate-buffered solution (PBS). The intensity was measured at excitation and emission wavelengths of 297 nm and 527 nm, respectively.

For SEM, we referred to Lan’s method and modified it slightly [15]. In total, 1 mL of P. fluorescens cultured overnight was treated differently (The treatment method is the same as that used in the growth curve of P. fluorescens experiment). P. fluorescens treated differently was cultured in a shaker for 6 h and washed with PBS. Later, a zinc plate was fixed with glutaraldehyde for 3.5 h. Finally, the zinc plate was gradient eluted with 30%, 50%, 70%, 90%, and 100% ethanol, and each concentration was placed for 15 min. Bacteria were observed after gold spraying. For CLSM, we referred to Yang’s method and modified it slightly [16]. P. fluorescens cultured overnight was treated differently (The treatment method is the same as that used in the growth curve of P. fluorescens experiment). P. fluorescens after different treatments was incubated at 28°C for 4 h, and its suspension was washed with the sodium chloride solution. The, 1.5 μL of dyes was added to 1 mL of P. fluorescens, kept in the dark for 15 min, and suspended in the sodium chloride solution. The intensity was observed at excitation and emission wavelengths of 480 nm and 490 nm, respectively, by CLSM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and electrochemical properties of RR-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
UV–vis DRS (SHIMADZU UV-3600 Plus) was used to record the optical absorption properties of the samples in the range of 200–800 nm. The PL spectrum was measured at 25°C with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F7000, Japan) to obtain the PL intensity of the prepared samples. The EPR of free radicals was detected using the Bruker A300-10/12 spectrometer. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), transient photocurrent density, and CV curve were measured using an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660E). The experimental details are described in the Supporting Information. The capability of the catalyst to produce active radicals is primarily affected by its band gap structure. To further investigate the band gap energies of RR-CDs, we referred to Jia’s method and modified it slightly. We calculated it using the following Equations (1) and (2) based on the UV–vis DRS analysis (Tauc plot) [17].
(1)

Where, α represents the absorption coefficient, hν represents photon energy, A represents a constant, and Eg represents band gap. Direct band gap: m = 1, indirect band gap, m = 4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of RR-CDs on the growth of P. fluorescens
ce:section.ce:para: We referred to Sun’s method and modified it slightly [13]. P. fluorescens/CV026 cultured overnight (1 mL) was collected in a 1.5-mL tube and centrifuged 5 min at 8554 g. The precipitate was resuspended in the 0.9% NaCl solution. The experiment was repeated thrice to collect the P. fluorescens suspension. After different treatments, 200 μL of the mixed solution was transferred into 96-well plates, with three replicates for each treatment. The growth curve was plotted using an automatic growth curve instrument (OD600, every 2 h). Different treatment groups were as follows: P. fluorescens/CV026 without visible light irradiation and RR-CDs (control), P. fluorescens with C6-HSL, P. fluorescens with visible light, P. fluorescens/CV026 with RR-CDs (20 μg mL−1), and P. fluorescens/CV026 with visible light irradiation and RR-CDs (20 μg mL−1). We referred to Pourmadadi’s method and modified it slightly [18]. In total, 1 mL of P. fluorescens cultured overnight was treated differently (The treatment method is the same as that used in the growth curve of P. fluorescens experiment in the anti-QS analysis part). The treated bacterial solution was centrifuged 5 min at 8554 g, to collect the bacterial suspension. The bacteria were stained with the propidium Iodide (PI) dye (1 h) and measured using a flow cytometer (4°C, dark).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of RR-CDs on the QS activity of P. fluorescens
ce:section.ce:para: We referred to Zhang’s method and modified it slightly [19]. CV026 cultured overnight with 50 μg mL−1 kanamycin monosulfate was inoculated into 10 mL of the LB broth medium at a volume ratio of 1:20. After mixing evenly, the plate was inverted and punched with an Oxford cup. Then, 100 μL of the RR-CDs-treated (light) P. fluorescens solution (including 5 μL of AHL) was added to the well, whereas the control was not treated with RR-CDs and light. The samples were fostered at 28°C for one day to observe the purple ring formed around the Oxford cup.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of RR-CDs on ECP of P. fluorescens
ce:section.ce:para: We referred to Vadakkan’s method and modified it slightly [20]. In total, 50 mL of skimmed milk with a concentration of 4.5% (w v−1) was sterilized at 110°C for 15 min. Immediately after sterilization, the milk was cooled using cold water and subsequently combined with 100 mL of agar with a concentration of 2.25% (w v−1), which was sterilized in an autoclave and cooled to 50°C. An Oxford cup was used to foster bacteria. In total, 1 mL of P. fluorescens cultured overnight was treated differently (The treatment method is the same as that used in the growth curve of P. fluorescens experiment in the anti-QS analysis part) and cultured for a day. Next, the bacterial liquid was centrifuged for 10 min at 14,000 g and filtered. In total, 200 mL of the filtrate was added to the previously described Oxford cup hole, and the petri dish was then fostered for two days at 28°C. Finally, the diameter of the proteolytic circle was measured using an automatic colony counter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of RR-CDs on motility of P. fluorescens
ce:section.ce:para: We referred to Jin’s method and modified it slightly [21]. Two types of media were prepared as follows: swarming medium (1% peptone, 0.5% NaCl, 0.5% agar, and 0.5% fructose) and swimming medium (1% tryptone, 0.5% NaCl, and 0.3% agar). Then, 1 mL of P. fluorescens cultured overnight was treated differently (The treatment method is the same as that used in the growth curve of P. fluorescens experiment in the anti-QS analysis part). After treatment, 100 μL of P. fluorescens was placed in the above-prepared media and fostered for a day. The diameter of bacterial movement and migration was measured using an automatic colony counting instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of RR-CDs on the P. fluorescens biofilm
ce:section.ce:para: For SEM, we referred to Yan’s method and modified it slightly [22]. A sterile zinc sheet (5 mm × 5 mm × 1 mm) was placed on a plate and the LB broth was added. Subsequently, 20 mL of the bacterial solution and varying concentrations of RR-CDs were added to the plate and fostered for two days. Zinc flakes were washed with the biofilm in sterile water. It was then transferred to a new 24-well plate, subjected to a 30-min stream of sterile air to dry the surface, and treated with 1 mL of pre-cooled 2.5% (v v−1) glutaraldehyde to fix the biofilm. After fixation, the zinc sheet was sequentially immersed in ethanol of 50%, 70%, 80%, 90%, and 100% for 5 min. Following dehydration and drying, the surface of the zinc sheet was coated with gold. Lastly, the biofilm on the surface of the zinc sheet was examined by SEM. For CLSM, we referred to Yu’s method and modified it slightly [23]. In a sterile glass bottom culture dish, the LB broth, 20 mL of the bacterial solution, and RR-CDs were added and fostered for two days. The bacterial solution was subsequently removed and rinsed gently with 0.01 mol/L PBS buffer thrice. Following this, 1 mL of 2.5% (v v−1) glutaraldehyde pre-cooled at 4°C was added (2 h). Once the fixative was removed, the bacteria were dried with sterile air for 30 min. Thereafter, the bacteria were dyed using 1 mL of 0.01% (w v−1) Acridine Orange (AO) and rinsed thrice with 0.01 mol/L PBS. Upon drying the culture dish, a drop of anti-fluorescence quenching sealing agent was added to completely cover the glass bottom. Finally, CLSM was used to observe the biofilm attached to the glass bottom using an CLSM (ex-wavelength: 488 nm, blocking filter wavelength: 515 nm). Determination of the amount of the biofilm: We referred to Li’s method and modified it slightly [24]. In total, 1 mL of P. fluorescens cultured overnight was treated differently (The treatment method is the same as that used in the growth curve of P. fluorescens experiment in the anti-QS analysis part). After inoculating 200 μL of the LB broth into 96-well plates, 10 μL of the treated P. fluorescens solution was added to the wells, and the plates were fostered for two days. After incubation, the P. fluorescens solution was gently sucked away, and P. fluorescens on the surface was rinsed by PBS. Finally, the solution was dried for 30 min. Subsequently, the bacteria were stained using 1 mL of 0.1% (w v−1) crystal violet solution for 20 min. The dye solution was then removed, and the bacterial solution was further dried by subjecting it to a stream of sterile air for 30 min. Then, 1 mL of 33% (v v−1) ethanoic acid was added to the samples to dissolve the biofilm. After mixing well and maintaining it for 5 min, the optical density was measured at 595 nm using a microplate reader.

We referred to Livak’s method and modified it slightly [25]. The activated overnight P. fluorescens was differently treated as follows: light, RR-CDs, RR-CDs with light, and RR-CDs with light (Contains 1 mmol/L of NAC; RR-CDs is 20 μg mL−1), and the control group was not treated with any of these. Then, the treated groups were inoculated in 100 mL of the LB broth at 1:100 (v v−1), cultured, and cultured overnight. At OD600 = 1, RNA was obtained from different treatment groups using EZ-10 Total RNA Mini-PrECP Kit. RNA was used as a template, rpoD of P. fluorescens was used as an internal reference gene, and pgaC, hutU, fliI, tyrB, motB, and aprE were used as target genes. qPCR was performed using Applied Biosystems. StepOneTM Software v2.3 automatically obtained the cycle threshold (Ct) value of each sample as well as calculated and analyzed the difference in target gene expression between the samples according to the 2-ΔΔCt method. Gene sequences are detailed in the Supplementary Material (Table S4).

The methodology of Cui et al. was used with slight modifications [12]. Fresh salmon fillets were cut into 5 cm × 5 cm salmon fillet blocks under sterile conditions. After different treatments, they were placed in a sterile clean preservation box and preserved at 4°C. Different treatment groups were as follows: control group, light group, 20 μg mL−1 RR-CDs group, rosmarinic acid (RosA) group (a common food preservative), and 20 μg mL−1 RR-CDs (visible light) group.

All values are represented as the mean ± standard deviation. Differences between the two groups were compared by t-test, and a one-way analysis of variance was performed to compare multi-group data using SPSS version 20. P < 0.05 was considered statistically significant, whereas P > 0.05 was considered not significant.

First-principles calculations were performed using the Vienna ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) [26]. Wherein the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) formalism of the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) was adopted to deal with the electron exchange–correlation term [27]. The energy cut-off of the plane wave basis was set to 500 eV, and the convergence threshold of energy and force was set to 1 × 10−5 Ha and 0.01 Ha/Å, respectively. The DFT-D3 method dealt with the van der Waals interactions [28]. The Brillouin zone integration was sampled by 1 × 1 × 1 Monkhorst-Pack k-points meshes [29]. The vacuum space was set to be 10 Å in the x, y, and z directions to separate the interactions between adjacent images.

Following a one-step hydrothermal method, yellow–green luminescent RR-CDs were synthesized using Rose Bengal and riboflavin as raw materials. The functional groups of RR-CDs were further analyzed using a Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer. The peaks at 3000–2850 cm−1 likely represent alkyl C-H stretching vibrations and carboxyl O-H stretching absorptions, respectively, and those at 1440–1340 cm−1 represent alkyl C-H bending vibrations (Fig. 2 a). Furthermore, the peaks at 1772, 1320–1210, and 937 cm−1 represent C=O stretching absorptions, C-O stretching vibrations, and extra-planar bending vibrations of O-H bonds, respectively, confirming the presence of carboxylic acids within the RR-CDs. The peak at 3388 cm−1 likely represents the intermolecular hydroxyl group and amino N-H stretching vibrations. The peak at 769–659 cm−1 indicates out-of-plane bending of hydroxyl groups, confirming their presence in the RR-CDs. The peaks at 1640–1560 and 900–650 cm−1 represent the deformation vibrations and out-of-plane bending oscillations of N-H, respectively, suggesting the presence of amino groups. The peaks at 645 and 600–500 cm−1 were attributed to C-Cl and C-I bonds in RR-CDs, respectively; Cl and I elements originate from Rose Bengal.

TEM revealed the spherical morphology of the RR-CDs, and the average size of the similarly sized RR-CDs was 3.1 ± 0.83 nm (Fig. 2b). Additionally, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images confirmed the 0.19-nm lattice of the RR-CDs, which is consistent with the characteristic crystal surface (101) [30]. The crystal morphology of RR-CDs was examined through XRD and the 2θ-value was 21.4° (Fig. 2c). Additionally, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) revealed the chemical constituents of the RR-CDs, with the five major elements being C, N, O, Cl, and I, accounting for 54.23%, 13.86%, 21.24%, 9.70%, and 0.97% (Fig. 2d). Figure S1 shows the XRS maps of different elements and the results are consistent with FTIR.

The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrum showing the optical characterization of RR-CDs (Fig. 2e) presented a distinct and well-defined absorption peak at approximately 211.0 nm, which is consistent with the π-π* transitions within the aromatic sp2 domain [31]. Additionally, characteristic peaks observed at approximately 300.0 and 500.0 nm confirmed the successful modification of Cl and I elements. Furthermore, the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of RR-CDs were 380.0 and 567.0 nm, respectively. Figure S2 shows the different excitation wavelength of RR-CDs.

The photostability of photocatalytic materials reflects the catalytic ability [32]. This study tested the photostability of the prepared RR-CDs exposed to simulated visible light at different times. The fluorescence intensity of the prepared RR-CDs was unchanged for 5.5 h, and the fluorescence intensity was above 85% for 10 h. Even after 14 h of irradiation, the fluorescence intensity could remain at about 81% (Fig. S3 a). This indirectly indicates that RR-CDs can produce ROS stably under light within 14 h [33]. Wang et al. used the prepared Fe-Cur composite nanoparticles to extend the shelf life of pork. The Fe-Cur composite nanoparticles can maintain light stability within 40 min, indicating that as long as the material remains stable within the time of action, the shelf life can be effectively extended [34]. The adaptability to face complex environments is a prerequisite for RR-CDs to be used in practical applications [35]. Salmon fillets may produce glucose, various amino acids, and various ions during the storage process. Therefore, we carried out stability experiments on RR-CDs to examine the state of RR-CDs under environmental conditions such as light, various ions, and amino acids. We selected 10 common ions and organic substances in the environment to be mixed with RR-CDs (Figure S3b). The fluorescence spectra showed that the fluorescence of the mixed solution did not change significantly after coexistence with various substances, indicating that the RR-CDs have good environmental stability.

To verify the in vitro bacterial inhibitory activity of RR-CDs, the rot-causing bacteria affecting seafood were treated with RR-CDs (12.5, 25, 50, 100, and 200 μg·mL−1) using the plate counting method. Notably, RR-CDs exhibited a wide range of inhibitory activity at lower concentrations (Figure S4). We chose P. fluorescens as the model bacterium and measured the MIC (50.0 μg·mL−1) of RR-CDs on P. fluorescens, And the growth curve proves it (Fig. 3a, Figure S6). The visible light inhibition observed may be closely linked to a novel antibacterial mechanism triggered by RR-CDs, potentially leading to a rapid increase in the ROS content and impeding pathogen resistance. This novel antibacterial strategy may be attributed to the abundant functional groups present on the surfaces of RR-CDs, facilitating favorable chemical properties that aid in bacterial penetration. Additionally, ROS can induce cellular oxidative harm via nonspecific metabolic routes and mitigate drug resistance [36].

The antibacterial ability of the RR-CDs was explored through CLSM. The control, pure RR-CDs, and pure light groups exhibited prominent green fluorescence. With increasing CD concentration, the red fluorescence intensified under visible light. The SEM images confirmed that P. fluorescens was unaffected by RR-CDs or visible light treatment (Fig. 3c). Untreated P. fluorescens presented a certain edge structure and intact cell wall. In contrast, exposure to visible light considerably changed the morphology of P. fluorescens with increasing CD concentration, leading to severe damage, atrophy, and leakage of cellular contents. Furthermore, following visible light treatment, the cell membrane permeability of P. fluorescens increased substantially compared with that of the control group (Fig. 3d). Reportedly, increased ROS content targets the double strands on the bases, damaging the nucleic acid skeleton, and thereby damaging the DNA [37].

Increasing evidence supports that ROS-mediated damage to cellular structure is a potent antimicrobial mechanism [37]. Compared with the control group, combining light irradiation with CD treatment notably increased the endogenous ROS level in the bacteria (Fig. 3e). The generation of additional ROS, driven by the electron-hole pairs produced by CD–light cotreatment, contributed considerably to the exceptional antibacterial activity of RR-CDs. Altogether, these findings show that RR-CDs under visible light exposure exhibit a notable antibacterial effect via ROS generation, resulting in bacterial cell membrane disruption and the release of cellular contents (Fig. 3f).

To further elucidate the catalytic potential of RR-CDs under visible light, UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) of RR-CDs, Rose Bengal, and riboflavin was performed. RR-CDs exhibited a broader absorption range, spanning from UV to visible light, with an absorption edge at approximately 750.0 nm (Fig. 4 a). Unlike riboflavin, RR-CDs exhibited a notable absorption tail in the visible range. The enhanced absorption of visible light is a key factor contributing to the improvement in photocatalytic activity [38].

Carrier separation and charge transfer efficiency are crucial factors for evaluating the catalytic activity. Generally, a higher photoluminescence intensity indicates poorer electron-hole separation efficiency in a material [39]. All samples presented narrow emission peaks within the 500–600 nm range (Fig. 4b). Notably, the peak intensity of RR-CDs was the lowest among all the samples, suggesting their superior carrier separation efficiency. The transient photocurrent response denotes the material charge transfer. RR-CDs exhibited considerably increased photocurrent, smaller Nyquist radius, and the highest electrochemical redox potential peak under visible light excitation compared with that of rose bengal and riboflavin, indicating superior photogenerated charge separation and transfer efficiency (Fig. 4c-e).

The conduction band position of RR-CDs was determined using the Tauc plot method based on UV–vis DRS analysis (Fig. 4a). RR-CDs exhibited optical absorption peaks in the visible region, representing the absorption edge in this spectral range, thus, making it the focal point in the Tauc plot analysis. The band gap of RR-CDs in the visible spectrum was 2.05 eV, which indicated oxygen vacancies in RR-CDs [40]. The flat band energy of RR-CDs was further analyzed through Mott–Schottky curve analysis (Fig. 4f). The linear segment at the peak was extended to identify the intersection point with the horizontal axis, and the flat band potential (Efb) of RR-CDs was determined to be −0.33 eV. Additionally, the conductivity consistently resided approximately 0.1 eV below the conduction band edge, resulting in the conduction band position of RR-CDs at −0.43 eV. The formula (3) used for the valence band is as follows:
(3)
E CB
=
E VB
+
E g
Accordingly, the valence band of RR-CDs was found to be −2.48 eV. These results indicate that RR-CDs developed in this study exhibited excellent light-response ability as a nanomaterial.

To identify active ROS, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline 1-oxide and (2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidin-1-yloxyl) were used as spin probes for detecting the ROS type, and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy was used for determining intensity [41]. When exposed to light, RR-CDs generated · O2 −, 1O2, and · OH, whereas no ROS signals were detected in the absence of light (Fig. 4g–i). Additionally, RR-CDs were tested for vacancies by EPR spectroscopy and presented a vacancy defect value at g = 2.0003 (Fig. 4j), further confirming the presence of oxygen vacancies [42]. Under visible light irradiation, RR-CDs produced 1O2 via oxygen vacancy-mediated energy transfer [43]. Moreover, the notable decrease in signal suggested the generation of electron holes on the surface of RR-CDs (Fig. 4k). The RR-CDs mixed with the bacterial solution can react with the oxygen molecules in the water to produce · OH, thereby exerting the bactericidal effect [44]. Altogether, these results suggest that visible light excites RR-CDs to produce ROS, such as · O2 −, 1O2, and · OH, via electronic transfer and energy transfer (Fig. 4l).

To understand the mechanism of ROS catalyzed by RR-CDs, the undergoing reason was explained in terms of the free energy difference and charge transfer based on the proposed catalytic mechanism. The free energy differences (ΔG) to estimate the catalytic activity have the following expression:
(4)
Δ G = Δ E + Δ
E
ZPE
- T Δ S
where ΔE, ΔE ZPE, T, and ΔS stand for the electronic energy difference, the zero-point energy difference, the room temperature (273.15 K), and the entropy difference, respectively. The vibration frequency calculations obtained the zero-point energy. In a vibration frequency model, adsorbate is released, and the substrate is fixed due to the insignificant vibration of the substrate. As shown in Fig. 5 a, when an O2 molecule is contacted and adsorbed on RR-CDs, 0.007 e- tends to transfer from RR-CDs to O2, indicating O2 acts as an electron acceptor by the Bader charge analysis (Fig. 5e). This process needs to overcome a low energy barrier with 0.48 eV. Then, the generation of · O2 − needs to overcome an extremely high energy barrier with 4.10 eV. However, the process of generation of 1O2 is exothermic with −0.08 eV, indicating the generation of 1O2 could be spontaneous at room temperature (Fig. 5c). In addition, the possibility of RR-CDs-mediated ̇OH production was also investigated. In general, · OH can be derived from H2O by two possible reaction pathways, which are listed below [45,46].
(5) *+H2O → *H2O
(6) *H2O → *H + · OH
(7) *H2O → *OH + H+ + e-
(8) *OH → * + · OH
where * represents an adsorption site of RR-CDs.*H2O, *H and *OH represent RR-CDs adsorbed H2O, *H and *OH, respectively. For simplicity of calculation, the H+-e- ion pair is treated as 1/2 H2. As shown in Fig. 5b and d, the H2O molecule is adsorbed on RR-CDs with the ΔG value of 0.31 eV. Meanwhile, the charge is depleted on RR-CDs, while the charge is accumulated on the H2O molecule, giving rise to 0.005 e- transferred from RR-CDs to H2O (Fig. 5f). Subsequent reactions may occur via two different pathways. One is that, as listed in equation (6), the *OH desorbs from RR-CDs and one ̇OH releases with an extremely high ΔG value of 4. 80 eV. The other is going through equations (7) and (8) successively, which required a lower Gibbs free energy of 2.48 eV and 0.09 eV, respectively. Compared with these catalytic reaction pathways, the RR-CDs-mediated catalytic reaction with O2 as the catalytic substrate generates 1O2 required only one step with the lowest ΔG value of 0.48 eV, demonstrating RR-CDs have much more tendency to catalyze 1O2 generation via O2 adsorption. Therefore, the RR-CDs can effectively induce the generation of 1O2 by catalyzing the decomposition of O2, resulting in high catalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The underlying mechanism of CD-mediated inhibition of QS
ce:section.ce:para: Strain CV026 can synthesize violacein from N-acyl-L-homoserine lactones (AHLs), if present in its surroundings, serving as an indicator of varying AHL levels and providing insights into the QS regulation of P. fluorescens [47]. Notably, a distinct purple ring was observed surrounding CD-treated pores, indicating the suppression of QS by RR-CDs (Fig. 6 a). Interestingly, the opaque purple ring suggested that RR-CDs at the corresponding concentration did not exhibit bactericidal ability. This indicates that the interference of RR-CDs with the violacein is primarily associated with their QS-inhibition activity, rather than antibacterial effects. Furthermore, antibacterial assays revealed no notable variations in growth curves and flow cytometry results among different samples, implying that the QS inhibition was independent of growth suppression (Fig. 6b, c, and Figure S5). Liu et al. have also tested the synthesis of AHL using the CV026 strain. These results highlight the potential of RR-CDs in QS inhibition, prompting further comprehensive investigations [48].

ECP generation promotes bacterial proliferation by providing nutrients by degrading proteins, and swarming and swimming are movement behaviors mediated by flagella, both of which are regulated through QS [49,50]. Compared with the control group, in the group treated with RR-CDs (visible light), P. fluorescens exhibited a notable reduction in its capacity to swarm, swim, and produce ECPs by 3.9, 1.9, and 2.6-fold, respectively (Fig. 6d–f). Although each group produced proteolytic circles, their diameters were considerably different (Fig. 6f), and that of the proteolytic circles created by the RR-CDs (visible light) treatment group was markedly small, indicating the ECP-repressive activity of RR-CDs in P. fluorescens.

Biofilm can enhance the invasive ability and drug resistance of bacteria, and it represents a common mechanism in QS regulation [51]. SEM images showed the dense biofilm formation on the bacterial surface, which facilitates the evasion from conventional antimicrobial agents (Fig. 7 a). Subsequent treatment with RR-CDs and RR-CDs (visible light) considerably reduced biofilm coverage on the bacterial surface. Particularly, in the RR-CDs (visible light) treatment group, ROS generation markedly reduced the amount of biofilm produced, thereby supporting the potential role of ROS in inhibiting biofilm formation. CLSM revealed evident biofilm coverage in both the control and visible light-treated groups, with densely packed living cells on these biofilms (Fig. 7b). However, following the treatment with RR-CDs (visible light), the density and structure of the biofilm were notably disrupted. The quantitative detection of the biofilm was consistent with the above experimental results (Fig. 7c and d). Altogether, RR-CDs showed potential as an effective antibacterial agent in inhibiting QS under visible light irradiation, leading to a suppression in the QS activity of P. fluorescens (Fig. 7e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural exploration and molecular docking of RR-CDs with LuxI/R
ce:section.ce:para: The complex nature of small molecule RR-CDs makes the characterization of their composition challenging. However, it has been widely acknowledged that their nanostructures feature amorphous shells and nuclei on the surface, with the formed surface groups considerably affecting the function of RR-CDs [52]. The effects of the QS system of P. fluorescens on bacterial spoilage capability have been validated. LuxR-type receptor proteins detect AHLs produced by homologous LuxI proteins, thus, initiating target gene expression and increasing AHL synthase LuxI expression, consequently establishing a positive feedback loop [53]. Herein, the potential chemical structure of RR-CDs was analyzed via high-performance liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (HPLC–MS) for docking analysis.

Homology modeling aims to predict the spatial structure of the target protein based on sequence homology with known structures and is characterized by its simplicity, speed, and relatively high accuracy [54]. The nucleic acid sequences of LuxR and LuxI from P. fluorescens PF-08 were extracted from previous studies. Homology modeling was performed using the SWISS-MODEL and AlphaFold Protein Structure Database platforms [55,56]. Template sequences showing high sequence identity (>30 %) were chosen to construct the three-dimensional models of LuxR and LuxI proteins. Finally, the most stable LuxR and LuxI protein structures were selected for molecular docking to minimize the errors. Global Model Quality Estimation (GMQE) scores and assesses the quality of the overall model, with a higher score corresponding to a superior model quality. In the SWISS-MODEL website modeling, templates A0A3M5MPU5.1.A, 3p2h.1.A, and 1ro5.1.A exhibited high consistency with the LuxI sequence, and templates 7r3i.1.B, 7r3i.1.A, and 7r3h.1.A exhibited the highest consistency with the LuxR sequence. Table S1 presents the GMQE scores of 0.96, 0.84, and 0.84 for A0A3M5MPU5.1.A, 7r3i.1.B, and 7r3i.1.A, respectively. Table S2 presents the HSP score of 1,028 and 1,240 for acyl-homoserine-lactone synthase (2) and LuxR family transcriptional regulator, respectively. The A0A3M5MPU5.1.1.A template protein (93.0%) and the LuxR family transcriptional regulator template protein (95.5%) exhibit the highest scores on the Ramachandran plot (Figure S7). Consequently, the A0A3M5MPU5.1.1.A and LuxR family transcriptional regulator templates were selected for modeling.

The QS system based on P. fluorescens comprises LuxI/R receptor proteins. AHLs bind to the receptor proteins as signal molecules and trigger the expression of specific QS target genes [57]. Herein, molecular docking was performed to elucidate the mechanism of RR-CDs underlying anti-QS and study potential interactions between RR-CDs and corresponding receptor proteins. The results for the docking of RR-CDs with LuxI/R modeling proteins (Table S3) revealed that numerous compounds on the surface of the RR-CDs could fit into the docking pocket of LuxI and LuxR, thereby forming remarkable interactions (Fig. 8 a, Figure S8, and Figure S9). The binding energies of the structures with the highest matching degree to LuxI and LuxR were −57.233 and −58.9833, respectively. The small molecule bound to the key residues of LuxI, namely arginine, tryptophan, and aspartic acid, via salt bridges and to the key residues of LuxR, namely tryptophan, tyrosine, phenylalanine, and aspartic acid, via the same interactions. The interaction between the small molecule and LuxI/R enhanced protein binding via van der Waals forces, hydrogen bonds, electrostatic interactions, and hydrophobic interactions.

To investigate the inhibitory effect of RR-CDs on QS at the molecular level, six related genes, namely pgaC, hutU, fliI, tyrB, motB, and aprE, were subjected to the qPCR analysis of P. fluorescens. The RR-CDs and RR-CDs + light groups inhibited the transcription of all test genes (Fig. 8b). RR-CDs + synergistic visible light inhibited the transcription of pgaC, tyrB, motB, and aprE by more than 50%. PgaC encodes a PGA-specific glycosyltransferase that regulates the synthesis of poly-N-acetylglucosamine, a major component of a biofilm matrix. RR-CDs + light inhibited the transcription of pgaC by 59%, thereby explaining the biofilm inhibitory activity [58]. Additionally, the decreased transcription levels of the key genes encoding urinary acid hydratase (hutU [59]), aromatic amino acid aminotransferase (tyrB [60]), and alkaline protease (aprE [61]) explained the reduction of extracellular proteolytic rings. Similarly, changes in swarming and swimming circles were attributed to the downregulated expression of the flagellar synthesis- and movement-related genes fliI and motB [23]. N-acetyl-L-cysteine (NAC) is often used as an ROS scavenger because it lowers ROS levels in the surrounding environment. In this study, after scavenging ROS, NAC significantly upregulated the expression of six genes, thereby further proving that RR-CDs inhibited the QS of P. fluorescens, as well as the synergistic effect of RR-CDs and ROS limited the QS to the greatest extent (Fig. 8b). Wang et al. analyzed the role of QSI PF2571MT in the A. veronii BY-8 gene at the molecular level and found that the rates of lysR, fliT, and serC inhibition were more than 50%, thereby explaining the QS inhibitory activity [62]. Herein, the binding model and qPCR suggested that the QS inhibitory activity was because of the binding of CDs to LuxR and LuxI, which inhibited the production of signaling molecules, and synergistic ROS blocked the expression of QS pathways and related genes (Fig. 8c). Wang et al. confirmed that high ROS levels produced under the light induced the expression of biofilm-related genes, and molecular docking showed that CG-KCDs interfered with biofilm production by binding to S. aureus beta-ketoacyl-acp synthase III [25].

Overall, when co-cultured with P. fluorescens, RR-CDs first bind to LuxI/R proteins through the interactions of various functional groups, inhibiting the secretion of AHLs, and affecting to a certain extent the eavesdropping of AHLs by LuxR. RR-CDs interfere with the normal QS activities of P. fluorescens, thus affecting the expression of downstream genes. Dou et al. analyzed the antibiotic sensitivity of the AHL-deficient AbS mutant and indicated that the reduction of AHL downregulated gene expression [63]. In addition, when exposed to visible light, RR-CDs excite oxygen in air and water to form various ROS, which act on DNA and genes. Zhu et al. treated Staphylococcus aureus with TMP-CQDs and reported a decrease in the contents of AddB, MetK, and LuxS, indicating the potential of TMP-CQDs to change DNA repair and QS [64]. Through qPCR experiments, it was further confirmed that the combined action of RR-CDs and ROS affected the expression of QS genes, resulting in a decrease in motility, a reduction in ECP production as well as preventing biofilm formation, thus comprehensively decreasing the spoilage ability of P. fluorescens.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Freshness maintaining properties of salmon fillets by RR-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and slices of the treated salmon fillets were analyzed and the total viable count (TVC), pH, Total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N), and 2-thiobarbituric acid (TBA) of the salmon fillets were measured to determine the preservation performance of RR-CDs. Changes were observed in the salmon with time (Fig. 9 a). With increasing time, the surface stripes of the salmon fillets gradually became blurred, white spots were observed, and a taste of decay was exuded, which were particularly evident in the control and light groups. Conversely, the fillets of the RosA and RR-CDs (visible light) group showed corruption after a longer period. Initially, salmon fillet tissues were closely arranged; however, owing to the invasion of bacteria and the decomposition of protein during spoilage, the salmon fillet tissues lost their elasticity, which increased the fiber gap (Fig. 9b). The bacteria grew slowly in the salmon fillets because of RosA and CD (visible light) treatments (Fig. 9c), and the two groups showed less damage to salmon fillet tissue fibers. The pH of fresh salmon fillets meat was about 6.3, and the pH of each group first decreased and then increased (Fig. 9d), which was attributed to an increase in lactic acid levels in the early stage of corruption. RosA and CD (visible light) treatments exerted little effect on these pH changes.

TVB-N accumulation is a crucial marker for determining the quality of meat and aquatic products because it is associated with the breakdown of nitrogen molecules by bacteria and proteolytic enzymes [65]. The initial TVB-N value of the salmon fillets was approximately 7.5 mg/100 g, a significant decrease from the permitted maximum value of 30 mg/100 g (Fig. 9e), which suggested that the quality of the samples was satisfactory. As time progressed, the TVB-N value of the control group increased sharply to 47.6 mg/100 g. After the CD (visible light) treatment, the TVB-N value of the salmon fillets slowly increased, which was correlated to bacterial QS inhibition by RR-CDs. Additionally, RosA treatment positively delayed the increase of TVB-N.

TBA is an indicator of lipid oxidation, and a very high TBA value is directly correlated to an unpleasant taste. During cold storage, the TBA values of each treatment group increased continuously (Fig. 9f); for instance, the TBA value of the control group increased rapidly, and on day 6, it surpassed 0.65 mg MDA kg−1. Conversely, the TBA value of the CD (visible light) treatment group markedly increased but the rate was slower, which indicated that RR-CDs (visible light) inhibited lipid oxidation to some extent. In summary, CD-mediated PDT inhibited QS and effectively prolonged the shelf life of salmon fillets by about 3 days (Fig. 9g).

To ensure the biological safety of RR-CDs in food preservation, we performed a CCK-8 cytotoxicity assay using HepG-2 cells as a model and revealed that the presence of RR-CDs enhanced the activity of HepG-2 cells within 400 μg mL−1. The 0.4 mg mL−1 RR-CDs did not significantly repress HepG-2 cell survival, and the cell survival rate reached about 100% (Figure S10a). Hemolysis assays further confirmed the lack of substantial toxicity of RR-CDs towards rat cells (Figure S10b).

In the present study, RR-CDs with photodynamic activity were synthesized. The HPLC-MS spectrometry analysis revealed multiple new structures formed on the CD surfaces at increased temperatures. The molecular docking results indicated that several structures could bind to LuxI/LuxR associated with the QS of P. fluorescens. The structures with the highest degree of compatibility were firmly bound to the residues neighboring the active pockets of the proteins via van der Waals forces and salt bridges, eventually affecting the synthesis and binding of AHLs to LuxR. The effects of RR-CDs on pgaC, hutU, fliI, tyrB, motB, and aprE transcription analyzed at the molecular level indicated that RR-CDs inhibited biofilm formation, motility, and extracellular protein production. Furthermore, the photodynamic stimulation of RR-CDs increased the concentrations of · O2 −, 1O2, and · OH via electron-hole separation, eventually intensifying the oxidative damage to P. fluorescens. The underlying antibacterial mechanism showed that when exposed to visible light, RR-CDs ruptured cell membranes and sharply increased ROS levels in bacterial cells. Subsequent SEM and CLSM corroborated that this abundant ROS substantially impeded P. fluorescens mobility, reduced extracellular protease production, and impeded biofilm formation. In conclusion, CD is a promising avenue for developing QS inhibitors with photodynamic capabilities, low cytotoxicity, and favorable biocompatibility; thus, CD is an excellent candidate for inhibiting the proliferation of spoilage bacteria.
